data.title: Allbirds updates guidance after last week's shock AI pivot
data.description:
Allbirds (BIRD) issued a guidance update on Tuesday ahead of the company's Q1 earnings report. The company estimates Q1 net revenue of $22.3M and adjusted EBITDA of $16.1M. A net loss of $19.6M is anticipated for the quarter, plus or minus $3M. The official Q1 report is expected to be released during the first two weeks of May.

Last week, Allbirds (BIRD) ripped a huge gain after announcing an unexpected pivot to being an AI infrastructure company. "The rise of AI development and adoption has created unprecedented structural demand for specialized, high-performance compute that the market is struggling to meet," stated the former shoe seller. The announcement arrived a few days after Allbirds (BIRD) had sold its shoe technology IP for $39M.

Allbirds (BIRD) traces its roots back to New Zealand in 2007 and was later developed by footballer Tim Brown into a wool-based shoe concept. Brown partnered with biotech engineer Joey Zwillinger in 2015, and the company launched its Wool Runner in 2016 with a direct-to-consumer model focused on simplicity and sustainability. The brand grew quickly by tapping into Silicon Valley’s appetite for minimalist, eco-friendly products, expanding into retail and new materials like eucalyptus fiber. Allbirds (BIRD) went public in November 2021, but its share price declined over the last four years after posting a long streak of unprofitable quarters.

Shares of Allbirds (BIRD) moved 3.5% higher in premarket action after rising 2.0% on Monday. The stock is well below its 2026 high of $24.31, but it has still more than doubled since the AI announcement. Short interest on BIRD stands at 17% of the total float based on the last update from the Nasdaq.

data.title: GE Aerospace beats estimates, stock gains as guidance holds
data.description:
GE Aerospace (GE) topped Wall Street expectations for the first quarter, sending shares up 3.4% in premarket trading Tuesday as the company posted stronger-than-expected revenue, earnings growth and reaffirmed its full-year outlook.

The maker of jet engines reported first-quarter revenue of $12.39 billion, ahead of analysts’ consensus estimate of $10.71 billion. Adjusted revenue rose 29% to $11.61 billion. Adjusted earnings came in at $1.86 a share, topping estimates of $1.60.

The results suggest GE Aerospace (GE) continues to benefit from robust demand for commercial aviation aftermarket services, a multiyear recovery in air travel and resilient defense spending. Investors have viewed the company as one of the cleaner industrial growth stories following its breakup from legacy General Electric operations, with a large installed engine base generating recurring high-margin service revenue.

Profit slips on GAAP basis

Net income fell to $1.93 billion, or $1.83 a share, from $1.97 billion, or $1.83 a share. 

Operating profit increased 18% to $2.53 billion, while operating profit margin narrowed to 21.8% from 23.8% a year ago.

Commercial engines drive growth

GE Aerospace (GE) said orders surged 87% to $23 billion in the quarter, helped by strong commercial demand. Commercial Engines & Services revenue climbed 34% to $8.92 billion, while Defense & Propulsion Technologies revenue rose 19% to $3.21 billion.

Chairman and Chief Executive Larry Culp in a statement said, “GE Aerospace had a strong first quarter with orders growing 87% and revenue up 29% supporting double-digit growth in earnings and free cash flow.”

Full-year outlook maintained

GE Aerospace (GE) maintained its 2026 guidance, calling for adjusted earnings of $7.10 to $7.40 a share and free cash flow of $8.0 billion to $8.4 billion, while saying results are trending toward the high end of the range.

For investors, the steady guidance may be as important as the quarterly beat. It signals management still sees enough strength in airline maintenance demand and defense markets to offset concerns about tariffs, slower global growth and geopolitical volatility.

data.title: Equifax beats Q1 street estimates
data.description:
• Equifax press release (EFX): Q1 GAAP EPS of $1.42 beats by $0.11.
  • Adjusted EPS attributable to Equifax was $1.86 in the first quarter of 2026, up 22% compared to the first quarter of 2025, beating estimates of $1.70.
  • Revenue of $1.65B (+14.6% Y/Y) beats by $30M.
  • Maintaining full year constant dollar revenue growth guidance due to uncertainty around interest rates and U.S. mortgage activity from the Iran conflict. Increasing full year reported revenue by $25 million and Adjusted EPS by $0.04 per share for the impact of foreign exchange

data.title: Small caps quietly match tech strength since March low
data.description:
U.S. small-cap stocks (IJR) (VB) (VBR) (VBK) (SCHA) (SPSM) (DFAS)are emerging as an unexpected driver of the market rally, alongside technology (QQQ), as performance broadens beyond a narrow group of leaders, according to Société Générale.

Cross-asset strategist Manish Kabra said small caps have delivered strong gains this year, with the Russell 2000 (IWM) up about 12% year-to-date and equal-weight small caps rising roughly 13%. That performance puts them on par with the tech-heavy Nasdaq (COMP.IND) since the market bottom in late March following the Iran-driven selloff.

Kabra argues the move reflects a deeper shift in the earnings cycle rather than a short-term bounce. The profit cycle for smaller firms bottomed in early 2025, and improving fundamentals are now feeding through into equity performance. As a result, the rally is no longer confined to a handful of large-cap names, but is instead becoming more broad-based across sectors and market capitalizations.

The bank highlighted that this broadening is visible not only in price action, but also in margins and earnings trends, suggesting a more durable underpinning for the advance. That stands in contrast to earlier phases of the bull market, which were heavily concentrated in a small group of megacap technology stocks.

data.title: 3M Non-GAAP EPS of $2.14 beats by $0.16, revenue of $6B in-line; reaffirms FY26 outlook
data.description:
• 3M press release (MMM): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $2.14 beats by $0.16.
  • Adjusted sales of $6B (+3.9% Y/Y) in-line,  with adjusted organic sales up 1.2% year-on-year.
  • Sales of $6.0 billion, up 1.3% year-on-year with organic sales down 1.4% year-on-year.
  • Cash from operations of $0.6 billion.
  • Adjusted free cash flow of $0.5 billion.

    • Full-year 2026 guidance3M reiterated the following full-year 2026 expectations.

    • Adjusted total sales growth1 of ~4 percent, reflecting adjusted organic sales growth1 of ~3 percent.  vs. estimated growth of 3.42% Y/Y
    • Adjusted operating income margin expansion1 of 70 bps to 80 bps.
    • Adjusted EPS1 in the range of $8.50 to $8.70. vs $8.65 consensus
    • Adjusted operating cash flow1 of $5.6 to $5.8 billion, contributing to >100 percent adjusted free cash flow conversion1.

data.title: D.R. Horton GAAP EPS of $2.24 beats by $0.10, revenue of $7.6B in-line
data.description:
• D.R. Horton press release (DHI): Q1 GAAP EPS of $2.24 beats by $0.10.
  • Revenue of $7.6B in-line (-2.2% Y/Y).
    • Home sales revenues of $7.0 billion on 19,486 homes closed
    • Net sales orders increased 11% to 24,992 homes with an order value of $9.2 billion

    • Based on the Company’s results for the first six months of fiscal 2026 and current market conditions, D.R. Horton is updating its guidance for fiscal 2026 as follows:

      • Consolidated revenues in the range of $33.5 billion to $34.5 billion vs $33.63B consensus
      • Homes closed by homebuilding operations of 86,000 homes to 87,500 homes

      The company is reiterating its fiscal 2026 guidance as follows:

      • Income tax rate of approximately 24.5%
      • Consolidated cash flow provided by operations of at least $3.0 billion
      • Share repurchases of approximately $2.5 billion
      • Dividend payments of approximately $500 million

data.title: Labor Secretary Chavez-DeRemer is latest Trump official to resign
data.description:
Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer is resigning from her post amid an internal investigation into allegations of misconduct, making her the third woman to exit the Trump administration in recent weeks.

Chavez-DeRemer "will be leaving the administration to take a position in the private sector," Steven Cheung, White House communications director, announced. He added that Keith Sonderling, deputy labor secretary, will take on the role of acting secretary.

The Labor Department's inspector general was investigating complaints of Chavez-DeRemer having an affair with a subordinate, drinking on the job, and visiting friends and family on the taxpayers' dime, the New York Post reported in January.

Her husband, Shawn DeRemer, was barred from the Labor Department's headquarters because of sexual assault allegations, The New York Times reported in February. Federal prosecutors closed the investigation into the matter without bringing charges.

Chavez-DeRemer's resignation follows two other high-profile exits from the Trump administration – Kristi Noem, who previously led the Department of Homeland Security, and Pam Bondi, former attorney general.
